description: Enumerations for point of view, focalization, consciousness representation, psychic distance, diegetic levels, and narrator typing.

pov_type.description: Point of view narrator type — who narrates, and in what grammatical person.

values.definition: The narrator uses 'I' and is a character within the story.
values.example_usage: Reliable or unreliable protagonist narrators; epistolary fiction.

values.definition: The narrator addresses the reader or a character as 'you'.
values.example_usage: Choose-your-own-adventure; experimental literary fiction; direct address.

values.definition: Third-person narration limited to the perceptions of one character per scene.
values.example_usage: Most contemporary literary and genre fiction.

values.definition: Third-person narration with access to multiple characters' thoughts and knowledge beyond any single character.
values.example_usage: Victorian novels; epic fantasy with multiple POV threads.

values.definition: Third-person narration with no access to any character's interior; camera-eye only.
values.example_usage: Hemingway-style; minimalist fiction; screenplay-adjacent prose.

focalization_type.description: Genette's focalization categories — who perceives, distinct from who narrates.

values.definition: Non-focalized (omniscient): the narrator knows more than any character.
values.example_usage: Omniscient authorial narrator with access to all interior states.

values.definition: Single character's perspective filters all narrated events in the scene.
values.example_usage: Close third-person; first-person; deep POV.

values.definition: Focalization shifts between different characters across scenes or within a scene.
values.example_usage: Multi-POV chapters; free indirect discourse switching.

values.definition: The same event is focalized through multiple characters' perspectives.
values.example_usage: Rashomon-style narration; he said/she said chapters covering the same event.

values.definition: Camera-eye focalization: no access to any character's interior state.
values.example_usage: Objective/behaviorist narration; Hemingway; screenplay-adjacent prose.

consciousness_mode.description: Dorrit Cohn's taxonomy of how prose fiction represents mental life.

values.definition: The narrator reports what a character thinks, using the narrator's own words and framing ('She felt humiliated').
values.example_usage: Narratorial summary of inner state; authorial mediation of consciousness.

values.definition: Direct thought presented in the character's own voice, usually tagged ('She thought: How dare he').
values.example_usage: Italicized direct thought; tagged interior monologue.

values.definition: Free indirect discourse (FID): character thought rendered in the narrator's grammar but the character's diction and perspective ('How dare he! What gave him the right?').
values.example_usage: The dominant mode of literary fiction; Jane Austen, Flaubert, James.

values.definition: A scene moves between multiple consciousness modes.
values.example_usage: Scenes that alternate psychonarration with narrated monologue.

psychic_distance.description: John Gardner's five-level scale of felt distance between reader and character consciousness.
psychic_distance.note: This is an integer field (1–5) in the schema, not a string enum. Values are documented here for reference.

values.definition: Maximally distant authorial narrator; generalized historical or sociological narration. ('It was winter of the year 1853...')
Named character introduced with authorial framing but without interiority. ('Henry Dowell was growing old...')
Character's perspective, but narrated through an intermediary narrator filter. ('He thought the town looked cold...')
Deep character filter: thoughts rendered in the character's diction and rhythm. ('Cold, yes, the town was cold...')
Stream of consciousness: direct unmediated thought, no narratorial distance. ('Cold. Bitter cold. God how he hated...')

diegetic_level.description: Genette's three narrative levels — where in the frame hierarchy the narration occurs.

values.definition: The outermost frame level: the narrator is outside the story world they narrate.
values.example_usage: Standard authorial narrator; frame story outer narrator.

values.definition: The primary story level: the narrator exists within the story world.
values.example_usage: Most narrative content; homodiegetic narrator who is also a character.

values.definition: A story-within-the-story level: narration occurring inside the intradiegetic level.
values.example_usage: A character telling a story to other characters; embedded tales; Scheherazade structure.

narrator_reliability_type.description: Booth/Nünning narrator reliability classification — how and why a narrator may diverge from the implied author's norms.

values.definition: Narrator's account aligns with the implied author's norms; the narrator's version of events can be trusted.
values.example_usage: Standard authorial narrator; unambiguous third-person narrator.

values.definition: Narrator misreports events — they lie, misremember, or have incomplete access to facts.
values.example_usage: A narrator who describes events they did not witness; a narrator who later contradicts themselves.

values.definition: Narrator misreads the meaning of events they accurately report.
values.example_usage: Stevens in Remains of the Day: accurately reports events but draws wrong conclusions.

values.definition: Narrator misjudges the moral weight or ethical significance of events.
values.example_usage: Humbert Humbert in Lolita: minimizes harm he causes.

values.definition: Narrator sincerely believes their own distorted account; unreliability is unconscious.
values.example_usage: A grieving narrator who cannot see their own role in events.

values.definition: Reliability depends on the reader's normative frame; the narrator reflects norms that some readers share and others reject.
values.example_usage: A narrator from a historical period with different moral assumptions.

narratee_type.description: Gerald Prince's narratee types — the fictional entity addressed by the narrator.

values.definition: A named or directly addressed narratee within the text ('Dear reader'; 'I am writing to you, Marcus...').
values.example_usage: Epistolary fiction; direct address narratives.

values.definition: A narratee constructed by the text's assumptions about what must be explained — present but not named.
values.example_usage: Most realistic fiction; the constructed ideal reader implied by expository choices.

values.definition: The narratee is a character within the story who visibly reacts to or questions the narrator.
values.example_usage: Frame narratives where the frame audience is shown reacting (Canterbury Tales).

values.definition: No specific narratee; the text addresses a universal unmarked reader position.
values.example_usage: Most omniscient third-person narration; standard genre fiction.

pov_role_type.description: A character's perceptual role in a specific scene — used in Character Scene State.

values.definition: This character's perception filters all events in the scene; their interior is accessible.
values.example_usage: The POV character.

values.definition: This character is present and acting in the scene but not the focalizer; their interior is not directly accessible.
values.example_usage: Any non-POV character who appears in the scene.

values.definition: This character is referenced or discussed in the scene but is not physically present.
values.example_usage: Characters talked about; ghost characters.

tone.description: The narrator's or implied author's attitude toward the subject matter — distinct from mood (atmosphere) and voice (register).

values.definition: The text's surface meaning differs from its intended meaning; distance between statement and reality is exploited.
Social or political critique delivered through irony, exaggeration, or mockery.
Mournful reflection on loss, decline, or the passing of what was valuable.
Elevated, musical language; emphasis on beauty and feeling over event.
High suspense; the reader awaits an outcome with dread or urgency.
A sense of foreboding or dark fate approaching.
Dry, understated irony delivered with apparent neutrality.
Celebratory, light, positive energy.
Intimate, emotionally warm, affectionate.
Absence of hope or redemption; cold despair.
Detached, observational, analytical — emotions withheld.
High energy; the reader is propelled forward by necessity.
Elevated, ceremonial register; social distance maintained.
Close, confessional, direct — feels like private address.
Playful, humorous, light; conflict resolved or mitigated by laughter.
Elevated grief; the weight of irreversible loss.
